annotations.label: Definition of 5

anchor.snippet: following 4 and preceding 6

annotations.note: The numeral 5 and its ordering follow from OfNat/numeral machinery but there is no dedicated declaration defining 5 as 'between 4 and 6'.

annotations.label: 5 as Fermat prime, Mersenne exponent, Fibonacci number

anchor.snippet: 5 is a Fermat prime

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Nat.fermatNumber (with fermatNumber_one = 5), mersenne, and Nat.fib, but no theorem asserting 5 is simultaneously a Fermat prime, Mersenne exponent, and Fibonacci number.

annotations.label: 5 as first congruent number and hypotenuse of smallest Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: 5 is the first congruent number

annotations.note: PythagoreanTriple is defined and classified, but congruent numbers are absent and no result identifies 5 as the smallest hypotenuse or first congruent number.

annotations.label: 5 as first safe prime and first good prime

anchor.snippet: 5 is the first safe prime

annotations.note: Neither safe primes nor good primes are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 11 and 5 as first sexy prime pair

anchor.snippet: 11 forms the first pair of sexy primes

annotations.note: Sexy primes (primes differing by 6) are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 5 as second of five known Fermat primes

anchor.snippet: of a total of five known Fermat primes

annotations.note: Nat.fermatNumber_one states fermatNumber 1 = 5 (so 5 is the second Fermat number), but Mathlib has no result about the count of known Fermat primes.

annotations.label: 5 as first Wilson prime

anchor.snippet: the first of three known Wilson primes

annotations.note: Wilson's theorem is formalized (Nat.prime_iff_fac_equiv_neg_one) but the distinct Wilson-prime condition p² ∣ (p−1)!+1 is not.

annotations.label: Pentagon

anchor.snippet: A shape with five sides is called a pentagon

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of polygons such as the pentagon.

annotations.label: Equilateral pentagon does not tile the plane

anchor.snippet: The equilateral pentagon is the first regular polygon

annotations.note: No result about regular pentagons failing to tile the plane exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dodecahedron has largest face among Platonic solids

anchor.snippet: The pentagon solid has the largest face

annotations.note: Platonic solids and the dodecahedron are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conic determined by five points

anchor.snippet: A conic is determined using five points

annotations.note: There is no theorem in Mathlib that a conic is determined by five points.

annotations.label: Pentagram

anchor.snippet: A pentagram , or five-pointed polygram , is a star polygon

annotations.note: Star polygons / pentagrams are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Five regular Platonic solids

anchor.snippet: There are five regular Platonic solids

annotations.note: Mathlib has the related ADE/sum-of-inverses classification (ADEInequality.classification) but no theorem enumerating exactly five Platonic solids.

annotations.label: Five Bravais lattices in the plane

anchor.snippet: The plane contains a total of five Bravais lattices

annotations.note: Bravais lattices and their classification are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform tilings from five regular polygons

anchor.snippet: Uniform tilings of the plane

annotations.note: Mathlib has generic tiling infrastructure (Combinatorics.Tiling) but no result about uniform tilings from regular polygons.

annotations.label: Pentagram diagonals divide in golden ratio

anchor.snippet: intersections of diagonals divide one another in the golden ratio

annotations.note: The golden ratio constant is defined (Mathlib.NumberTheory.Real.GoldenRatio) but the pentagram-diagonal geometric fact is not.

annotations.label: 5-cell (hypertetrahedron)

anchor.snippet: A hypertetrahedron , or 5-cell, is the 4 dimensional analogue

annotations.note: Affine.Simplex generalizes the n-simplex (the 5-cell is a 4-simplex) but the regular 4-polytope structure of the 5-cell is not specifically formalized.

annotations.label: Five mirror symmetry point group families in 4D

anchor.snippet: There are five fundamental mirror symmetry point group families

annotations.note: No classification of 4D mirror-symmetry point group families exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Five compact hyperbolic Coxeter groups of rank 5

anchor.snippet: There are also 5 compact hyperbolic Coxeter groups

annotations.note: General Coxeter systems are formalized (Mathlib.GroupTheory.Coxeter) but not the classification of compact hyperbolic rank-5 Coxeter groups.

annotations.label: 5 as center of Luoshu magic square

anchor.snippet: 5 is the value of the central cell of the first non-trivial normal magic square

annotations.note: Magic squares are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum of five non-zero squares

anchor.snippet: can be expressed as the sum of five non-zero squares

annotations.note: Lagrange's four-square theorem (sum_four_squares) is formalized, but the distinct result on sums of five non-zero squares is not.

annotations.label: Five Ramsey classes of permutations

anchor.snippet: There are five countably infinite Ramsey classes of permutations

annotations.note: Ramsey classes of permutations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 5 as only odd untouchable number (conjecture)

anchor.snippet: 5 is conjectured to be the only odd , untouchable number

annotations.note: Untouchable numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Odd Goldbach conjecture

anchor.snippet: Every odd number greater than five is conjectured to be expressible as the sum of three prime numbers

annotations.note: The ternary Goldbach conjecture is absent; Mathlib's 'Goldbach's theorem' (coprime_fermatNumber_fermatNumber) is an unrelated result about Fermat numbers.

annotations.label: Odd numbers as sum of at most five primes

anchor.snippet: every odd number greater than one is the sum of at most five prime numbers

annotations.note: The result that every odd number > 1 is a sum of at most five primes is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: K5 is non-planar

anchor.snippet: all graphs with four or fewer vertices are planar

annotations.note: Graph planarity is not formalized in Mathlib (planar graphs appear only as a TODO comment in VertexColoring).

annotations.label: Kuratowski's theorem

anchor.snippet: a finite graph is planar if and only if it does not contain a subgraph

annotations.note: Mathlib's Kuratowski file concerns the metric-space Kuratowski embedding, not the graph-planarity characterization.

annotations.label: Five complex exceptional Lie algebras

anchor.snippet: There are five complex exceptional Lie algebras

annotations.note: All five exceptional Lie algebras (e₆, e₇, e₈, f₄, g₂) are defined via the Serre construction, but there is no theorem asserting exactly five exist.

annotations.label: Five Mathieu groups in happy family

anchor.snippet: The five Mathieu groups constitute the first generation

annotations.note: Mathieu groups are not formalized in Mathlib ('Mathieu' appears only as an author name).

annotations.label: Centralizer of order-5 element in Monster group

anchor.snippet: A centralizer of an element of order 5 inside the largest sporadic group

annotations.note: The Monster group and sporadic groups are not formalized in Mathlib.
